patterns.name: VLAN / Zone Isolation
patterns.basis: IEC 62443 zone/conduit model; NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 Section 5.3 (network segmentation for ICS)

conditions: Device is network-reachable and lateral movement risk exists.
Patch is delayed or vendor-managed.
constraints: Requires network change window and clinical workflow validation.
DICOM, HL7, and vendor remote-support paths must remain functional.

steps.action: Define target VLAN/VRF/zone
steps.details: Create or identify a dedicated VLAN/VRF/zone for the affected device class. Document target subnet and routing policy.

steps.action: Move ports / WiFi profiles to target VLAN
steps.details: Reassign switch ports or WiFi SSID profiles to the target VLAN. Confirm uplink ACLs permit required peer traffic only.

steps.action: Approve segmentation plan and document residual risk
steps.details: Review proposed VLAN topology. Record which flows are blocked, which remain permitted, and residual risk posture in the remediation packet.

steps.action: Coordinate downtime and validate modality workflows
steps.details: Schedule maintenance window with clinical staff. After move, validate DICOM connectivity, HL7 interfaces, and vendor remote support path.

evidence: Network diagram or change record showing VLAN assignment
Before/after routing reachability test results (ping, traceroute, or firewall flow log)
Clinical/HTM validation signoff confirming device functionality post-move

steps: Move switch ports back to original VLAN assignment.
Restore original routing/ACL rules on uplinks.
Notify clinical owners that rollback occurred and re-validate workflows.

safety_notes: Validate all clinical workflows (DICOM, HL7, vendor remote support) before and after the move.
Never execute during active patient procedures without explicit clinical approval.
Coordinate with HTM/CE to confirm device remains operational post-change.

patterns.name: ACL / Firewall Allowlist
patterns.basis: NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 Section 5.1 (firewall policies for ICS); CISA ICS-CERT recommended practice for defense-in-depth

conditions: Issue is known exploited or high risk, and a patch is not immediately available.
Full isolation is not feasible but traffic flows can be tightly controlled.
constraints: Requires knowledge of all required peer IPs/FQDNs and ports.
Must not block time sync, DNS, or vendor-required update channels.

steps.action: Implement ACL/firewall allowlist policy
steps.details: Create firewall or switch ACL entries permitting only required flows (peer IPs, ports, protocols). Default deny all other ingress and egress.

steps.action: Review allowlist rationale and record in packet
steps.details: Verify that permitted flows are minimally necessary. Record allow-rule rationale in the remediation packet with citations to advisory source.

steps.action: Validate device functionality and vendor support paths
steps.details: Confirm that clinical workflows, DICOM/HL7 interfaces, and vendor remote-support connections are intact post-ACL deployment.

evidence: Firewall or ACL rule export (or change ticket attachment)
Flow tests confirming allowed traffic succeeds and blocked traffic fails
Monitoring confirmation showing no unexpected egress (SIEM or flow log)

steps: Disable or remove the ACL/firewall policy.
Revert to the previous rule set or remove newly added deny rules.
Notify infosec and clinical owners; document reason for rollback.

safety_notes: Be careful not to block time sync (NTP), DNS, or vendor-required update/telemetry channels.
Test in a maintenance window if possible; have rollback ready before applying.
Confirm vendor support access remains functional before closing the change.

patterns.name: NAC Device Policy
patterns.basis: Common healthcare network defense practice; IEC 62443 device identity and authentication requirements

conditions: Facility has a NAC platform (e.g., Cisco ISE, Aruba ClearPass).
Affected devices are unmanaged or require posture enforcement.
constraints: Requires NAC platform and device profiling capability.
Hard quarantine must not be applied to life-safety devices without clinical signoff.

steps.action: Create or adjust NAC policy for the device class
steps.details: Define a NAC policy targeting the device class, MAC OUI, or certificate identity. Assign quarantine, restricted, or monitored VLAN based on posture.

steps.action: Define policy intent
steps.details: Decide on policy posture: quarantine (block), restricted VLAN (limited access), or alert-only (monitor without blocking). Document rationale.

steps.action: Coordinate port changes with clinical owners
steps.details: Notify clinical stakeholders of NAC policy changes. Validate that affected devices can still communicate with required peers after policy activation.

evidence: NAC policy screenshot or configuration export
Test device authentication result showing correct VLAN assignment
VLAN assignment confirmation log or dashboard screenshot

steps: Revert NAC policy to previous state or remove the new policy rule.
Remove any quarantine VLAN assignments from affected devices.
Confirm devices reconnect to their original VLAN and communicate normally.

safety_notes: Avoid applying 'hard quarantine' to life-safety or life-sustaining devices without explicit clinical signoff.
Alert-only mode is safer for initial deployment on clinical devices.
Coordinate with HTM/CE before any policy that could interrupt device connectivity.

patterns.name: Vendor Remote Access Restriction
patterns.basis: CISA ICS-CERT best practice for remote access to ICS/medical devices; FDA postmarket guidance Section VI.B (compensating controls)

conditions: Device is vendor-managed or vendor-supported and requires remote access.
Exploit vector involves remote access or unauthenticated external connections.
constraints: Must not break emergency vendor support capabilities.
Exceptions require explicit documented approval.

steps.action: Require vendor remote access through controlled method
steps.details: Mandate that all vendor sessions route through an approved jump host or VPN with MFA where possible. Communicate requirement to vendor support team.

steps.action: Restrict remote access IP ranges, ports, and time windows
steps.details: Implement firewall rules permitting remote access only from vendor IP ranges, to required ports, during approved maintenance windows.

steps.action: Coordinate vendor access scheduling and document case/ticket
steps.details: Schedule vendor remote sessions in advance. Capture session start/end times, purpose, and any changes made. Log vendor case/ticket reference.

evidence: Jump host or VPN access log showing approved vendor session
Time-window firewall policy evidence (rule export or change ticket)
Vendor case or ticket reference ID documenting the session purpose

steps: Restore prior remote access paths only with explicit infosec approval.
Document the reason for rollback in the remediation packet.
Re-assess need for restrictions if rollback is permanent.

safety_notes: Never completely block emergency vendor support for life-safety devices — use documented exceptions with explicit approval instead.
MFA and jump host requirements may need vendor coordination; allow adequate lead time.
Retain session logs for audit and incident response purposes.

patterns.name: Vendor Case: Open and Track
patterns.basis: FDA postmarket guidance (vendor coordination for device vulnerabilities); Practitioner experience from healthcare security operations

conditions: System is vendor-managed with no customer admin access, or patching requires OEM service.
Customer cannot apply patch independently due to contractual or technical restrictions.
constraints: Vendor responsiveness and SLA may vary.
Compensating controls (segmentation, ACL) should be applied while awaiting vendor remediation.

steps.action: Open vendor case and request patch/mitigation timeline
steps.details: Contact vendor support via official channel. Provide device model/serial, firmware version, and describe the vulnerability (CVE, advisory link). Request patch or mitigation timeline.

steps.action: Provide security context and required risk controls
steps.details: Supply KEV status, known exploitation details, and applicable CISA advisory reference. Communicate urgency and request written confirmation of remediation timeline.

steps.action: Provide remediation plan and validation steps
steps.details: Vendor delivers patch, firmware update, or compensating control procedure with test/validation checklist.

steps.action: Track case until closure; document risk acceptance if delayed
steps.details: Maintain tracking record with vendor case ID, dates, and updates. If vendor timeline exceeds risk tolerance, initiate GOVERNANCE_RISK_ACCEPTANCE.

evidence: Vendor case or ticket ID
Vendor-provided remediation notes or patch release announcement
Closure confirmation and validation signoff from HTM/CE

steps: N/A — this is a process pattern; no technical rollback required.
Re-open case if vendor-provided remediation introduces new issues.

safety_notes: Escalate immediately when patient care is impacted or exploitation is confirmed in the wild.
Do not close the case until validation of the vendor fix is complete.
Pair with compensating controls (SEGMENTATION, ACL) while awaiting vendor action.

patterns.name: Apply Patch / Update / Firmware
patterns.basis: FDA premarket guidance (software validation); CISA ICS-CERT advisory remediation procedures; Practitioner experience for clinical device patching workflows

conditions: A patch, update, or firmware fix is available from the vendor.
Patching is feasible within clinical and operational constraints.
constraints: Requires approved maintenance window and clinical downtime plan.
Backup and restore plan must be in place before patching life-safety systems.
Never patch life-safety systems without a downtime and validation plan.

steps.action: Apply patch per OEM procedure (vendor-managed devices)
steps.details: Vendor applies the patch or firmware update following their validated service procedure. Customer should observe and receive documentation of changes made.

steps.action: Apply patch per OEM procedure (customer-managed devices) and run validation tests
steps.details: Apply the patch during the approved maintenance window. After patching, execute the OEM-provided modality validation checklist. Document pass/fail status for each test.

steps.action: Record patched version and retire compensating controls
steps.details: Capture before/after version evidence (screenshots, command output). Update the remediation packet. Remove or document expiry of any compensating controls that are no longer needed.

evidence: Before/after version proof (screenshot or version query output)
Change record or patch ticket
Completed validation checklist signed by HTM/CE

steps: Execute OEM-supported rollback procedure if available.
Restore from pre-patch backup image where applicable.
Re-apply compensating controls (ACL, segmentation) until stable version is confirmed.
Document rollback event and root cause in the remediation packet.

safety_notes: Never patch life-safety or life-sustaining systems without a documented downtime and validation plan.
Confirm backup/restore is tested and available before beginning.
Schedule during lowest-clinical-risk window; have clinical team on standby.
For vendor-managed patches, obtain written confirmation of what was changed.

patterns.name: Risk Acceptance (Time-Bound)
patterns.basis: NIST SP 800-39 (risk management); Practitioner experience for healthcare governance with clinical operational constraints

conditions: Vendor has not released a patch and timeline is unknown.
Clinical operational constraints prevent applying available mitigations.
Device is end-of-life with no viable remediation path.
constraints: Must be time-bound with a defined review date.
Must document all compensating controls currently in place.
Requires explicit leadership approval — not infosec-only.

steps.action: Draft risk acceptance with residual risk statement
steps.details: Document the vulnerability, why remediation is not currently feasible, what compensating controls are active, and the residual risk level. Propose a review date.

steps.action: Acknowledge patient care considerations
steps.details: Clinical operations reviews and acknowledges the risk acceptance, confirming awareness of patient care implications and any workflow changes needed as compensating controls.

steps.action: Obtain leadership approval with review date and conditions
steps.details: Escalate to appropriate leadership (CISO, CIO, or clinical leadership per policy). Capture approval in ticketing system with review date and trigger conditions for re-evaluation.

evidence: Signed or ticket-approved risk acceptance document
Review date recorded in tracking system
Compensating controls documented with owner and expiry

steps: Re-open the remediation packet when a viable patch or mitigation becomes available.
Rescind the risk acceptance and transition to an active remediation pattern.

safety_notes: Risk acceptance must be time-bound — never indefinite without re-review.
Identify trigger conditions that would require immediate re-evaluation (e.g., active exploitation confirmed).
Compensating controls must be documented and verified as active at time of acceptance.

patterns.name: Clinical Downtime Notice
patterns.basis: Common healthcare network defense practice; FDA postmarket guidance Section VI (coordinated disclosure and clinical communication)

conditions: Any change requiring device downtime or workflow changes for clinical systems.
Security remediation will affect clinical staff workflows or patient care delivery.
constraints: Patient care takes priority over implementation speed.
Emergency changes require explicit clinical and leadership approval.
Fallback procedures must be documented before downtime begins.

steps.action: Communicate planned downtime and workflow changes to clinical staff
steps.details: Draft and distribute downtime notice to affected clinical departments. Include: system/device affected, scheduled window, expected duration, fallback procedures, and contact for questions.

steps.action: Confirm fallback procedures and acknowledge notice
steps.details: Clinical operations confirms receipt, acknowledges fallback workflows, and provides sign-off that the scheduled window is acceptable for patient care operations.

steps.action: Include security rationale and urgency level
steps.details: Add security context to the notice: vulnerability severity, exploitation status, and why the change cannot be further delayed. Helps clinical leadership make informed decisions.

steps.action: Confirm technical readiness before window
steps.details: Verify all technical prerequisites are in place: test environment validated, rollback procedure ready, team on standby. Confirm go/no-go with change coordinator.

evidence: Downtime notice sent with acknowledgement receipts from clinical stakeholders
Downtime window recorded in change management system
Post-change validation signoff confirming device and workflow restoration

steps: Reschedule the change if clinical operations cannot support the planned window.
Notify all stakeholders of the reschedule and updated timeline.
Re-confirm fallback procedures remain adequate for the extended interim period.

safety_notes: Patient care always takes priority over implementation speed.
Use emergency change procedures only with explicit clinical and leadership approval.
Ensure fallback (paper-based, manual) procedures are documented and practiced before the window.

patterns.name: Enhanced Detection and Monitoring
patterns.basis: NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 Section 6.2 (monitoring and detection for ICS); CISA ICS-CERT defense-in-depth recommended practice

conditions: Vulnerability cannot be immediately patched or segmented.
Exploit activity is possible and early detection is needed.
Compensating controls are in place but verification of their effectiveness is required.
constraints: Requires IDS/IPS infrastructure or SIEM with relevant rulesets.
Alert tuning may be needed to avoid false-positive fatigue on clinical networks.
Monitoring rules should not introduce latency on clinical data flows.

steps.action: Deploy or update IDS/IPS signatures for the vulnerability
steps.details: Add detection signatures targeting the specific CVE or attack pattern. Use vendor-provided signatures (Snort, Suricata) or create custom rules matching the exploit vector described in the advisory.

steps.action: Increase logging on affected device network segments
steps.details: Enable enhanced flow logging, packet capture triggers, or NetFlow/sFlow collection on switch ports and firewall zones serving the affected devices. Ensure logs are forwarded to SIEM.

steps.action: Create SIEM correlation rules and alert thresholds
steps.details: Build SIEM rules to alert on anomalous traffic to/from the affected device class — unusual ports, unexpected source IPs, high connection rates, or protocol anomalies. Set escalation thresholds appropriate for clinical environment.

steps.action: Establish monitoring review cadence until remediation
steps.details: Assign daily or shift-based review of detection alerts for the affected device segment. Document review schedule and escalation path. Continue until patch is applied or risk is formally accepted.

evidence: IDS/IPS signature deployment confirmation (rule ID, deployment date)
SIEM alert rule configuration screenshot or export
Sample alert test showing detection fires on simulated traffic
Monitoring review schedule documented with assigned owners

steps: Disable or remove custom IDS/IPS signatures if they cause false positives affecting clinical operations.
Revert enhanced logging to baseline levels to reduce storage/performance impact.
Remove SIEM correlation rules and notify monitoring team of rollback.

safety_notes: Monitoring rules must not introduce latency or packet loss on clinical network segments.
Tune alert thresholds to avoid alarm fatigue — high false-positive rates reduce detection effectiveness.
Coordinate with clinical engineering before deploying inline IPS (vs passive IDS) on medical device segments.
This pattern is a compensating control — it detects but does not prevent exploitation.

patterns.name: Credential Hardening
patterns.basis: NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 Section 5.2 (identification and authentication for ICS); FDA postmarket guidance (authentication and access controls)

conditions: Advisory mentions default credentials, hardcoded passwords, or authentication bypass.
Vulnerability involves weak or missing authentication on device management interfaces.
Device has factory-default accounts that have not been changed since deployment.
constraints: Credential changes on vendor-managed devices may require vendor coordination.
Some medical devices do not support password changes — vendor confirmation required.
Service accounts used by clinical applications must be coordinated before rotation.

steps.action: Audit device accounts and identify default or weak credentials
steps.details: Inventory all management accounts on affected devices. Identify factory-default passwords, shared credentials, and accounts without password expiry. Cross-reference with vendor advisory for specific hardcoded credentials.

steps.action: Coordinate credential changes with vendor if device is vendor-managed
steps.details: Contact vendor support to confirm supported password change procedure. Some devices require vendor-specific tools or firmware settings to change credentials. Document vendor case ID and approved procedure.

steps.action: Change default passwords and rotate service credentials
steps.details: Change all default and factory passwords to unique, strong credentials per facility policy. Rotate service account credentials used for device integration. Store new credentials in approved secrets manager.

steps.action: Disable unused administrative accounts
steps.details: Disable or remove administrative accounts not required for clinical operation or vendor support. Document which accounts remain active and their business justification.

steps.action: Validate device functionality after credential changes
steps.details: After credential changes, validate all clinical workflows, DICOM/HL7 interfaces, and vendor remote-support connections. Confirm service accounts used by integration systems still authenticate successfully.

evidence: Account audit report showing default accounts addressed
Password change confirmation (change ticket or screenshot of account settings)
Disabled account list with business justification for remaining active accounts
Clinical validation signoff confirming device functionality post-change

steps: Restore previous credentials from documented backup if new credentials break clinical workflows.
Re-enable disabled accounts only with explicit infosec approval and documented justification.
Notify vendor support if vendor-managed credentials need reversal.

safety_notes: Never change credentials on life-safety devices without a validated rollback procedure.
Coordinate service account changes with all dependent clinical applications before rotation.
Some devices may become inoperable if credentials are changed without following the vendor procedure — always confirm with vendor first.
Document all credential changes in the remediation packet for audit trail.

patterns.name: Disable Unused Services and Protocols
patterns.basis: IEC 62443-3-3 SR 7.7 (least functionality); CISA ICS-CERT recommended practice for attack surface reduction

conditions: Advisory affects a specific service or protocol that is not required for clinical operation.
Device exposes unnecessary network services that increase attack surface.
Vulnerability is in a component or feature that can be disabled without clinical impact.
constraints: Clinical engineering must confirm which services are required for device operation.
Some services may be required for vendor remote support — confirm before disabling.
Disabling services on FDA-cleared devices may require vendor guidance to maintain clearance status.

steps.action: Identify unnecessary services and protocols on affected devices
steps.details: Scan or review device configuration to enumerate running services, open ports, and enabled protocols. Cross-reference with the advisory to identify which vulnerable service can be disabled.

steps.action: Confirm which services are required for clinical operation
steps.details: Review service inventory with clinical engineering. Identify which services are required for patient care workflows, DICOM/HL7 communication, and vendor support. Document the minimum required service set.

steps.action: Disable unnecessary services following vendor procedure
steps.details: Disable identified unnecessary services using the vendor-documented procedure. If no vendor procedure exists, disable via device management interface during a maintenance window with rollback plan ready.

steps.action: Verify attack surface reduction and document changes
steps.details: Rescan or verify that disabled services are no longer accessible. Document before/after service state in the remediation packet. Confirm the vulnerable service identified in the advisory is no longer exposed.

evidence: Before/after port scan or service inventory showing disabled services
Clinical engineering signoff confirming no impact to clinical workflows
Change record documenting which services were disabled and why
Vendor confirmation that disabling the service does not void device clearance (where applicable)

steps: Re-enable disabled services using vendor procedure or device management interface.
Verify clinical workflows resume normally after re-enabling services.
Document reason for rollback and re-assess alternative mitigations.

safety_notes: Never disable services on life-safety devices without clinical engineering confirmation that the service is not required.
Some seemingly unnecessary services (e.g., SNMP, Telnet) may be used by biomedical monitoring or asset management systems — verify before disabling.
Disabling services on FDA-cleared devices should be coordinated with the vendor to ensure continued regulatory compliance.
Maintain a documented record of the original service configuration for rollback purposes.
